agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration between two bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — to enforce stock availability at order placement time. Currently these contexts are deliberately decoupled: orders are placed without checking warehouse stock. The feature request introduces a cross-context dependency that fundamentally changes both contexts' autonomy.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration: Warehouse Management ↔ Sales/Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"Non-negative integer in Warehouse context representing physical quantity of a Product available in the warehouse."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer request to purchase one or more products, composed of OrderLines. Currently accepted unconditionally in Sales context."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order referencing a Product ID and a requested Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An item that exists in both contexts — identified by ID, but potentially carrying different semantics (catalog item in Sales, physical inventory unit in Warehouse)."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — a concept that would need to exist if stock is checked at order time: a temporary hold on StockLevel to prevent overselling between check and fulfillment."},{"term":"Blocked Order","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — an order that was rejected or held due to insufficient stock. The feature request does not clarify the lifecycle of such an order."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — a real-time signal exposed to the Sales team showing whether a product has sufficient stock. Latency, granularity, and source of truth are unspecified."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for any Product must remain >= 0 at all times within the Warehouse context.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical stock cannot be negative. This is a hard constraint of physical reality and data integrity.","testable_assertion":"No operation in the Warehouse context may reduce StockLevel below 0; any attempt must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order consists of one or more OrderLines, each referencing a Product ID and a positive Quantity.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"An order without lines or with zero/negative quantities is structurally invalid and represents corrupt data.","testable_assertion":"Order creation must reject orders with zero OrderLines or any OrderLine with Quantity <= 0."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"When an order is placed, each OrderLine's requested Quantity must not exceed the available stock for that Product.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a new business policy, not a physical constraint. A VP could decide to allow backorders, partial fulfillment, or pre-orders — all of which would relax this rule.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X has StockLevel 5, when an Order is placed with an OrderLine for Product X Quantity 6, then the order must be blocked."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Stock availability checks must account for all in-flight orders (reservations) — not just the raw StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without accounting for reserved stock, two concurrent orders could each see StockLevel 5 and each order 5 units, resulting in overselling and a StockLevel integrity violation.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X has StockLevel 10 and 7 units are already reserved by pending orders, when a new order requests 4 units, then available stock is 3 and the order must be blocked."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The Sales context must not directly write to or enforce invariants within the Warehouse context. Cross-context communication must go through a defined integration mechanism.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Bounded context autonomy is an architectural invariant. If Sales directly manipulates StockLevel, both contexts lose independent evolvability and a single failure cascades across domains.","testable_assertion":"No Sales-context operation directly modifies StockLevel; all stock queries and reservations go through an anti-corruption layer or domain events."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Product has StockLevel of N in Warehouse and no existing reservations","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for that Product with Quantity <= N","then":"The order is accepted and stock is reserved (available stock decreases by Quantity for subsequent orders)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Product has StockLevel of N in Warehouse and existing reservations totaling R","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for that Product with Quantity > (N - R)","then":"The order is blocked and the customer is informed that stock is insufficient"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An Order contains multiple OrderLines across different Products","when":"Any single OrderLine exceeds available stock for its Product","then":"The entire Order is blocked (atomic check — no partial acceptance unless explicitly designed)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A blocked Order exists and stock is subsequently replenished","when":"The Sales team views the blocked Order","then":"The stock indicator reflects the updated availability (but the order does NOT auto-unblock without explicit action)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders that individually would succeed but collectively exceed stock","when":"Both orders are submitted concurrently","then":"Exactly one is accepted and one is blocked — no overselling occurs"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The Warehouse context is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer attempts to place an Order","then":"The system behavior is well-defined (fail-open with warning, or fail-closed with rejection — this must be decided)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Concurrent orders for the last unit of stock","expected_behavior":"Only one order succeeds; the other is blocked. Requires pessimistic locking or reservation-based concurrency control."},{"scenario":"Order placed, stock reserved, then order is cancelled","expected_behavior":"Reserved stock must be released back to available StockLevel. Failure to release creates phantom reservations that permanently reduce sellable inventory."},{"scenario":"Stock received from supplier while orders are being placed","expected_behavior":"Newly received stock should become available for in-flight orders. The stock indicator should reflect the increase without stale reads."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Sales but not yet in Warehouse (new product, no stock record)","expected_behavior":"Order must be blocked. Missing stock record should be treated as StockLevel 0, not as an error that crashes the order flow."},{"scenario":"Warehouse context is down or network partition between contexts","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — fail-open (accept order, risk overselling) vs fail-closed (reject order, lose sale). This is a critical business decision."},{"scenario":"Partial stock availability across multiple OrderLines","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — block entire order, or allow partial fulfillment? Current spec implies full block but business may want flexibility."},{"scenario":"Stock indicator shows 'available' but stock is consumed between indicator refresh and order submission","expected_behavior":"The indicator is eventually consistent; final enforcement must happen at order placement time. Indicator is advisory, not a guarantee."},{"scenario":"Reservation timeout — customer places order, stock is reserved, but order is never confirmed or paid","expected_behavior":"Reservations must have a TTL to prevent indefinite stock lockup. TTL duration is unspecified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling (costly backorder/cancellation costs) but risks lost revenue if stock checks are too aggressive or stale. Incorrect reservation logic could lock up sellable inventory. The 'fail-open vs fail-closed' decision during outages directly trades revenue against fulfillment cost.","operational":"Introduces a hard runtime dependency between two previously independent contexts. Warehouse outages now cascade to Sales — order placement is blocked if stock cannot be checked. Requires new monitoring, SLAs between teams, and incident response coordination. Reservation lifecycle (create, release, expire) adds operational complexity.","user":"Customers see a real-time stock indicator — sets an expectation of accuracy that eventually consistent systems may not meet. Blocked orders need a clear UX: is the order rejected outright, or held for later? Sales team needs to understand that the indicator is advisory, not a guarantee.","risk":"HIGH — This is a cross-context coupling that introduces distributed system failure modes: network partitions, stale reads, race conditions on concurrent orders. Without a reservation concept, overselling will occur under load. Without TTLs on reservations, inventory will leak. The spec does not address any of these, creating significant implementation risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may now be rejected at placement time instead of accepted and later back-ordered. UX must clearly communicate why and what to do next."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains a stock indicator (positive) but loses the ability to unconditionally accept orders (negative). Needs training on new order states (blocked, pending stock)."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Their StockLevel data now has a direct customer-facing SLA. Data accuracy and system uptime requirements increase significantly."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Must design and maintain a cross-context integration layer, reservation system, and handle distributed system failure modes that didn't exist before."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Fewer oversold orders reduces fulfillment costs, but overly aggressive blocking may reduce top-line revenue. Need metrics to track both."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The feature request introduces a fundamental cross-context integration but specifies almost none of the critical design decisions. The words 'automatically block' and 'real-time' each carry multiple valid interpretations. The absence of a reservation concept, failure mode policy, and blocked-order lifecycle makes it impossible to define complete acceptance criteria. Multiple ambiguities remain that would lead to different architectures depending on resolution.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant is clear and well-defined","Concurrent access requires some form of reservation to prevent overselling","Bounded context autonomy must be preserved — Sales cannot directly modify Warehouse state","The stock indicator must be treated as advisory, with enforcement at order time"],"low_confidence_areas":["Reservation TTL / expiry policy is completely unspecified","Fail-open vs fail-closed during Warehouse unavailability","Whether blocked orders are rejected permanently or held for retry","Partial fulfillment policy for multi-line orders"],"assumptions":["Stock check is all-or-nothing per Order (no partial fulfillment) unless told otherwise","The 'stock available indicator' is eventually consistent and advisory","A reservation concept will be introduced even though the spec doesn't mention it","Product IDs are consistent across both contexts (shared ID namespace)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Automatically block' is undefined. Does this mean: (A) reject the order outright and inform the customer, (B) accept the order into a 'blocked/pending' state visible to the Sales team for manual resolution, or (C) prevent order submission entirely via the UI? Each implies a different Order lifecycle and different domain events.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Hard reject — order is not created, customer gets an error","(B) Soft block — order is created in a 'Blocked' state, Sales team can act on it when stock arrives","(C) UI prevention — disable order submission when indicator shows insufficient stock"],"recommendation":"Option B (soft block) — it preserves the order intent, gives the Sales team actionable data, and avoids losing customer demand signal. Hard reject loses information; UI-only prevention is insufficient without server-side enforcement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' has no latency or consistency requirements defined. In a distributed system, true real-time is impossible; the business must define acceptable staleness. A 30-second delay vs a 5-minute delay leads to fundamentally different integration patterns (event-driven vs polling).","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering","Warehouse Team"],"options":["Sub-second via synchronous API call to Warehouse on each page load","Seconds-stale via event-driven projection (domain events from Warehouse update a read model in Sales)","Minutes-stale via periodic polling/batch sync"],"recommendation":"Event-driven projection (seconds-stale) — it decouples the contexts at runtime, provides near-real-time UX, and doesn't create a synchronous dependency that makes Sales unavailable when Warehouse is down."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No failure mode policy specified. When the Warehouse context is unreachable, should Sales fail-open (accept the order, risk overselling) or fail-closed (reject the order, lose the sale)? This is a business-value tradeoff, not a technical decision.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Finance / Revenue","Warehouse Team"],"options":["Fail-open: accept orders when stock cannot be verified, flag for manual review","Fail-closed: reject orders when stock cannot be verified, show unavailability message","Circuit-breaker with fallback: use last-known stock levels with a staleness warning"],"recommendation":"Fail-open with flagging — revenue preservation is typically more valuable than preventing occasional overselling, especially if overselling cost is manageable. But this MUST be an explicit business decision, not an engineering default."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No reservation lifecycle defined. If stock is 'held' when an order is accepted, when is it released? On cancellation, on payment timeout, on fulfillment? Without TTLs, abandoned orders permanently lock inventory.","affected_stakeholders":["Warehouse Team","Customers","Finance / Revenue"],"options":["Reservations expire after a fixed TTL (e.g., 15 minutes for unpaid orders)","Reservations persist until explicit cancellation or fulfillment","Reservations are soft (advisory) and final check happens at pick/pack time"],"recommendation":"Fixed TTL with explicit release on cancellation — prevents inventory leakage while giving customers a reasonable window. TTL duration should be set by the business, not engineering."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration between two bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — to enforce stock availability at order placement time. Currently these contexts are deliberately decoupled: orders are placed without checking warehouse stock. The feature request introduces a cross-context dependency that fundamentally changes both contexts' autonomy.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration: Warehouse Management ↔ Sales/Orde

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this domain integration scenario between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management bounded contexts.

This is a classic cross-context integration problem. The feature request wants to couple Sales directly to Warehouse, which has significant domain implications.

Key issues:
- Currently Orders are placed WITHOUT checking Context A (explicit design)
- Now they want to "automatically block" orders based on stock — this couples the contexts
- "Real-time stock available indicator" implies synchronous reads across context boundaries
- "Insufficient stock" is ambiguous — what does it mean exactly?

This is an ambiguity scenario. The feature request uses vague terms and can be interpreted multiple ways. 

The real problem is that "automatically block," "real-time stock," and "insufficient stock" each have multiple valid interpretations — does blocking happen before or after order placement, what latency is acceptable for stock visibility, and are we checking reserved inventory or just physical counts? The cross-context communication pattern itself is also undefined.

Escalation type: `ambiguity`
